title: Tensor/Matrix: implicit conversions
analysis:
Task 231 adds implicit type conversion rules to the compiler's type checking infrastructure. The type system already has Type::Tensor { rank, n, quantity } (from task 228) and Type::Matrix { m, n, quantity } will exist after task 229 is merged. Currently, type checking uses strict equality (==) everywhere: overload resolution (line 605), trait conformance (line 4183), and field composition (line 4640). The only soft rule is the dead-code `type_compatible()` function (line 554) which handles Int->Real widening.

The task requires four conversion rules:
1. Vector<N,Q> <-> Tensor<1,N,Q>: A rank-1 tensor with matching N and quantity is interchangeable with a Vector.
2. Scalar<Q> <-> Tensor<0,N,Q> for any N: A rank-0 tensor (scalar) with matching quantity is interchangeable with a Scalar, regardless of N (N is meaningless for rank-0).
3. Tensor<2,N,Q> -> Matrix<N,N,Q>: A rank-2 square tensor implicitly promotes to an NxN matrix (one-way only).
4. Matrix -> Tensor: NOT implicit (must produce compile error if attempted).

Implementation approach:
- Add a `pub fn implicitly_converts_to(from: &Type, to: &Type) -> bool` function that encodes all four rules directionally.
- Update `type_compatible()` to delegate to `implicitly_converts_to()` (bidirectional check).
- Wire `implicitly_converts_to()` into trait conformance (line 4183: actual->expected) and field composition (line 4640: codomain->domain).
- Keep overload resolution exact-match only (changing it would create ambiguity between overloads accepting Vector vs Tensor<1>).

Note: The compiler cannot currently resolve Vector/Tensor/Matrix from source type annotations (resolve_type_name only handles Scalar/Bool/Int/Real/String/Length/Mass). So integration tests that compile full source programs with these types are not yet possible. The tests will call `implicitly_converts_to()` directly with programmatically constructed Type values. The trait conformance and field composition wiring is mechanical (replacing `!=` with function call) and the conversion logic is independently tested.

Type representations used in conversions:
- Vector<3,Scalar[m]> = Type::Vector { n: 3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Scalar { dimension: LENGTH }) }
- Tensor<1,3,Scalar[m]> = Type::Tensor { rank: 1, n: 3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Scalar { dimension: LENGTH }) }
- Scalar[m] = Type::Scalar { dimension: LENGTH }
- Tensor<0,N,Scalar[m]> = Type::Tensor { rank: 0, n: N, quantity: Box::new(Type::Scalar { dimension: LENGTH }) }
- Matrix<3,3,Real> = Type::Matrix { m: 3, n: 3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Real) } (from task 229)
- Tensor<2,3,Real> = Type::Tensor { rank: 2, n: 3, quantity: Box::new(Type::Real) }

prerequisites.description: Merge task/228 branch (Tensor type/value) into this worktree. Type::Tensor { rank, n, quantity } and Type::tensor(rank, n, q) factory must be available. Already confirmed present in current worktree.

Merge task/229 branch (Matrix type/value) into this worktree. Type::Matrix { m, n, quantity } and Type::matrix(m, n, q) factory must be available. Must add Value::Matrix variant and exhaustive match arms. Currently NOT in worktree — requires merge of task/229 branch.

steps.description: Write failing tests in implicit_conversion_tests.rs for Vector<N,Q> <-> Tensor<1,N,Q> bidirectional conversion. Tests: (a) implicitly_converts_to(Vector{3,Real}, Tensor{1,3,Real}) == true, (b) implicitly_converts_to(Tensor{1,3,Real}, Vector{3,Real}) == true, (c) implicitly_converts_to(Vector{3,Length}, Tensor{1,3,Length}) == true with Scalar quantity, (d) implicitly_converts_to(Vector{2,Real}, Tensor{1,3,Real}) == false (mismatched N). Tests FAIL because implicitly_converts_to() does not exist yet.

Add `pub fn implicitly_converts_to(from: &Type, to: &Type) -> bool` to crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs. Initial implementation: return true for identity (from == to), Vector{n,q} -> Tensor{rank:1,n,q} (matching n and quantity), and Tensor{rank:1,n,q} -> Vector{n,q} (reverse). Return false for all other cases. Remove #[allow(dead_code)] from type_compatible if it references this function.

Write failing tests for Scalar<Q> <-> Tensor<0,N,Q> bidirectional conversion. Tests: (a) Scalar{LENGTH} -> Tensor{0,3,Scalar{LENGTH}} == true, (b) Tensor{0,3,Scalar{LENGTH}} -> Scalar{LENGTH} == true, (c) Scalar{ANGLE} -> Tensor{0,5,Scalar{ANGLE}} == true (different N), (d) Scalar{LENGTH} -> Tensor{0,3,Scalar{ANGLE}} == false (mismatched dimension), (e) Type::Real -> Tensor{0,3,Real} == true (dimensionless), (f) Tensor{0,2,Real} -> Type::Real == true. Tests for (e,f) FAIL because Scalar<->Tensor rule not yet implemented.

Extend implicitly_converts_to() with Scalar<->Tensor<0> rules. Add match arms: (a) from is any type Q, to is Tensor{rank:0, n:_, quantity:Q} where *quantity == *from -> true (any type can be a rank-0 tensor's element). (b) from is Tensor{rank:0, n:_, quantity:Q}, to is any type where *quantity == *to -> true. This handles Scalar, Real, Int as the quantity type. The N parameter is ignored for rank-0 tensors.

Write failing tests for Tensor<2,N,Q> -> Matrix<N,N,Q> one-way conversion and Matrix->Tensor rejection. Tests: (a) Tensor{2,3,Real} -> Matrix{3,3,Real} == true, (b) Tensor{2,3,Length} -> Matrix{3,3,Length} == true, (c) Matrix{3,3,Real} -> Tensor{2,3,Real} == false (REJECTED — Matrix->Tensor is not implicit), (d) Tensor{2,3,Real} -> Matrix{3,4,Real} == false (non-square mismatch), (e) Tensor{2,3,Real} -> Matrix{4,4,Real} == false (N mismatch), (f) Tensor{1,3,Real} -> Matrix{3,3,Real} == false (wrong rank). Tests FAIL because Matrix rule not implemented.

Extend implicitly_converts_to() with one-way Tensor<2>->Matrix rule. Add match arm: from is Tensor{rank:2, n, quantity}, to is Matrix{m, n:mn, quantity:mq} where n==m && n==mn && quantity==mq -> true. No reverse arm for Matrix->Tensor — the default false return handles rejection.

Write comprehensive negative and edge case tests. Tests: (a) identity: implicitly_converts_to(Type::Real, Type::Real) == true, (b) Int->Real: implicitly_converts_to(Int, Real) == false (Int->Real widening is NOT an implicit tensor conversion — it's a separate concern in type_compatible), (c) Point<3,Real> -> Tensor{1,3,Real} == false (Point is NOT Vector), (d) Vector{3,Real} -> Matrix{3,3,Real} == false (no Vector->Matrix shortcut), (e) Tensor{3,2,Real} -> anything-unexpected == false, (f) Tensor{0,3,Real} -> Tensor{1,3,Real} == false (different ranks, not covered by any rule), (g) unrelated types: Bool -> Tensor{...} == false.

Review and fix any edge cases revealed by step-7 tests. Ensure identity check is first (from == to), then specific conversion rules, then default false. Should mostly pass already from steps 2/4/6 implementation.

Write tests for type_compatible() integration with implicit conversions. Tests: (a) type_compatible(Tensor{1,3,Real}, Vector{3,Real}) == true (bidirectional), (b) type_compatible(Vector{3,Real}, Tensor{1,3,Real}) == true, (c) type_compatible(Real, Int) == true (existing Int->Real widening preserved), (d) type_compatible(Tensor{2,3,Real}, Matrix{3,3,Real}) == true, (e) type_compatible(Matrix{3,3,Real}, Tensor{2,3,Real}) == true (type_compatible is symmetric — checks both directions). Tests FAIL because type_compatible() still only checks exact match + Int->Real.

Update type_compatible() to use implicitly_converts_to(). New logic: (1) if from == to, return true; (2) if Int->Real widening (existing), return true; (3) if implicitly_converts_to(param_ty, arg_ty) || implicitly_converts_to(arg_ty, param_ty), return true; (4) return false. Remove #[allow(dead_code)] annotation. This makes type_compatible bidirectional (either direction of implicit conversion suffices).

Wire implicitly_converts_to() into trait conformance check at line ~4183. Replace `if actual_type != expected_type` with `if !implicitly_converts_to(actual_type, expected_type)`. Direction: actual (structure member) converts TO expected (trait requirement). This allows a structure to provide Tensor<1,3,Q> where trait requires Vector<3,Q>, or Vector where Tensor<1> is required, but NOT Matrix where Tensor is required.

Wire implicitly_converts_to() into field composition type check at line ~4640. Replace `if inner_field.codomain_type != outer_field.domain_type` with `if !implicitly_converts_to(&inner_field.codomain_type, &outer_field.domain_type)`. Direction: inner codomain converts TO outer domain. This allows field composition where inner returns Vector<3,Q> and outer expects Tensor<1,3,Q>, etc.

design_decisions.decision: implicitly_converts_to() is a directional function (from -> to), not symmetric
design_decisions.rationale: The Tensor<2,N,Q> -> Matrix<N,N,Q> rule is one-way only. A directional API naturally expresses this asymmetry. Callers that need bidirectional checking (like type_compatible) call it both ways. Callers that need directional checking (trait conformance: actual->expected, field composition: codomain->domain) call it once in the correct direction.

design_decisions.decision: Overload resolution stays exact-match only — implicit conversions NOT applied there
design_decisions.rationale: Adding implicit conversions to overload resolution would create ambiguity: fn f(x: Vector<3,Real>) and fn f(x: Tensor<1,3,Real>) would both match a Vector argument. The existing exact-matching design (from task 171) deliberately avoids this. Implicit conversions are applied at type-compatibility boundaries (trait conformance, field composition), not at dispatch boundaries.

design_decisions.decision: Scalar<Q> <-> Tensor<0,N,Q> applies to any quantity type Q, not just Type::Scalar
design_decisions.rationale: The task notation Scalar<Q> uses 'Scalar' loosely to mean 'a single value'. A rank-0 tensor with quantity Real is conceptually just a Real. Matching on the quantity type (*quantity == *from) handles Type::Scalar{dim}, Type::Real, Type::Int naturally without special-casing each.

design_decisions.decision: implicitly_converts_to() is pub for direct testing from integration tests
design_decisions.rationale: The function encodes important policy (which conversions are allowed). Making it pub allows thorough testing from the crate's tests/ directory using programmatically constructed Type values, without needing to compile full source programs. Currently, the compiler cannot resolve Vector/Tensor/Matrix from source type annotations (resolve_type_name doesn't handle them), so source-level integration tests aren't possible yet.

design_decisions.decision: Trait conformance and field composition wired without dedicated integration tests
design_decisions.rationale: The wiring is mechanical — replacing `actual_type != expected_type` with `!implicitly_converts_to(actual, expected)`. The conversion logic is independently and thoroughly tested via direct calls. Source-level integration tests will become possible when a future task adds Vector/Tensor/Matrix to resolve_type_name().

design_decisions.decision: type_compatible() checks BOTH directions for implicit conversion
design_decisions.rationale: type_compatible(a, b) answers 'can a and b be used interchangeably?' It calls implicitly_converts_to() in both directions. This is appropriate for contexts where the direction is ambiguous (e.g., testing if two types are assignment-compatible). Directional contexts (trait conformance, field composition) call implicitly_converts_to() directly.

reuse.what: Test pattern: parse source -> compile -> inspect diagnostics/templates
reuse.how: Follow the pattern: let parsed = reify_syntax::parse(source, ModulePath::single("test")); let compiled = reify_compiler::compile(&parsed); then inspect compiled.diagnostics for errors.

reuse.what: Type factory methods for constructing test types
reuse.how: Use Type::vec3(q), Type::tensor(rank, n, q), Type::matrix(m, n, q), Type::length(), Type::Scalar { dimension: DimensionVector::LENGTH }, Type::Real etc. to construct test types cleanly.
Preserve the existing function's role and extend it. It already handles identity and Int->Real widening. Add implicitly_converts_to() delegation without removing existing rules.
Replace `if actual_type != expected_type` with `if !implicitly_converts_to(actual_type, expected_type)`. Same error message format, just a broader compatibility check.
Replace `if inner_field.codomain_type != outer_field.domain_type` with `if !implicitly_converts_to(&inner_field.codomain_type, &outer_field.domain_type)`. Same error format.
